skill.name: sandbox-configurator
skill.description: Configure Claude Code sandbox settings with network, file system, command, and environment boundaries.
skill.summary: Build safer sandbox policies for Claude Code development workflows.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: The static external-command and weak-cryptography findings are false positives caused by markdown fences, command names, and descriptive security text. No malicious code, prompt injection, or data exfiltration was found, but the skill can recommend weaker sandbox settings such as Docker exclusions, Unix sockets, local binding, and no-sandbox mode.

medium_findings.title: Sandbox Boundary Relaxation Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends options that can reduce isolation, including Git and Docker exclusions, Unix socket access, local binding, and a no-sandbox level. These are legitimate configuration choices, but they increase exposure if used outside trusted environments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly describe weaker sandbox modes and exclusions. The skill frames them as optional and warns about risk, so the concern is elevated but not malicious.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Environment Variable Handling
medium_findings.description: The enterprise example asks users to add NPM_TOKEN and COMPANY_API_KEY as environment variables. The validation checklist says environment variables should not contain secrets, but the example can still encourage risky secret placement.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token and API key names are explicit. The later checklist mitigates the issue, so confidence is high for the risk pattern but lower for exploitability.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby and shell backtick detections occur inside markdown code fences and examples. No runnable script file or dynamic command execution path was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are markdown delimiters around JSON, YAML, and prompt examples. There is no executable Ruby or shell source in the scanned files.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections point to descriptive words and headings, not MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic code. No evidence of weak cryptographic implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain normal prose such as description, design, headings, and cascade references. I found no cryptographic algorithm usage in those locations.

low_findings.title: Static Reconnaissance Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The network and system reconnaissance detections refer to access-analysis labels and direct-system-access warnings. They do not contain scanning commands, discovery commands, or network probing behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited graph line connects workflow nodes, and the markdown line warns about direct access. No reconnaissance command or execution path is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Exclusion Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The example configuration excludes Git and Docker from sandbox controls. This can be necessary for workflows, but it weakens command isolation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The configuration values are explicit and security-relevant. The risk depends on user environment and whether excluded commands are needed.

dangerous_patterns.title: No-Sandbox Option
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents direct system access as a supported level, even though it labels it high risk. Users could select this mode in trusted but still exposed environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The no-sandbox path is clearly present and marked risky. It is not malicious because it is disclosed as a cautionary option.

content.value_statement: Teams need sandbox settings that match real development workflows without removing important isolation. This skill helps define Claude Code sandbox policies for network, file, command, Docker, and environment access.
actual_capabilities: Recommends sandbox levels from maximum security to development mode.
Defines trusted network domains for registries, GitHub, and internal services.
Explains Docker, Unix socket, Git, and local binding trade-offs.
Produces settings guidance for Claude Code sandbox configuration.
Provides validation checklists for sandbox behavior and security requirements.
Documents security decisions and workflow-specific exceptions.
limitations: It does not enforce sandbox settings by itself.
It can recommend weaker settings when users request broad development access.
It does not verify whether domains or environment variables are safe.
It assumes users understand their local threat model.

use_cases.title: Harden open source workspaces
use_cases.description: Create restrictive sandbox policies for reviewing unfamiliar repositories with minimal network and command access.

use_cases.title: Support enterprise development
use_cases.description: Map internal registries, trusted domains, and build requirements into a documented sandbox configuration.

use_cases.title: Balance full-stack workflows
use_cases.description: Allow local servers, Docker, and Git while documenting the isolation trade-offs.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a safe baseline
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use sandbox-configurator to recommend a maximum-security Claude Code sandbox for an unfamiliar repository.

prompt_templates.title: Configure full-stack access
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure a balanced sandbox for a full-stack app that needs npm installs, Git operations, Docker, and a local dev server.

prompt_templates.title: Review current settings
prompt_templates.scenario: Security review of an existing workspace
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current Claude Code sandbox settings and identify unnecessary network, command, file system, or environment access.

prompt_templates.title: Design enterprise policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a custom enterprise sandbox policy with internal registries, approved domains, Docker requirements, and documented exception rationale.

output_examples.input: Set up a safe sandbox for reviewing an unknown package.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends maximum security, public registry access only, no local binding, and minimal command exclusions.

output_examples.input: Allow Docker and a local development server.
output_examples.output: The skill proposes balanced settings, explains Unix socket and local binding risk, and lists validation steps.

output_examples.input: Audit my sandbox settings for open source work.
output_examples.output: The skill identifies broad trusted domains, unnecessary exclusions, and environment variables that should be removed or narrowed.

best_practices: Start with maximum security and add only necessary exceptions.
Document every trusted domain, command exclusion, and local binding decision.
Keep secrets out of sandbox configuration unless a secure secret mechanism is used.
anti_patterns: Using no-sandbox mode for unfamiliar repositories.
Adding wildcard trusted domains without a clear business need.
Excluding Docker, Git, or shell commands for convenience alone.

faq.question: Does this skill change settings automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides configuration guidance and validation steps for Claude Code sandbox settings.

faq.question: Is no-sandbox mode safe?
faq.answer: No. The skill labels direct system access as high risk and recommends it only for completely trusted environments.

faq.question: Can it support Docker workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains Docker command exclusions and Unix socket access, including their security trade-offs.

faq.question: Can I use custom internal registries?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill supports custom trusted domains for enterprise registries and internal documentation services.

faq.question: Does it protect secrets?
faq.answer: It warns users to avoid secrets in environment variables, but it does not provide secret storage or scanning.

faq.question: Which tools are supported?
faq.answer: The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
